summary: In this interview, Iris Murdoch defends the traditional novel as an inherently comic form that reflects ordinary human life and freedom. She strongly criticizes Jacques Derrida and post-structuralism, arguing that their linguistic determinism strips individuals of meaning, intimidates young writers into abandoning traditional storytelling, and dangerously fragments genres and philosophy.

principles.statement: The traditional novel is fundamentally a comic form.
principles.rationale: Human life, even in terrible situations, is full of jokes. The traditional novel reflects this ordinary human life, its worries, and its profound funniness.
principles.source_quote: the traditional novel is a look at human life, it is about people, their worries, what makes them happy, what makes them sad, and so on. And human life depends very much on the comic.

principles.statement: Funniness is an essential aspect of human freedom.
principles.rationale: Humor is a natural part of free human life, whereas totalitarian atmospheres actively destroy it.
principles.source_quote: Funniness is also, I think, an aspect of freedom, something that totalitarian atmospheres can destroy. But ordinary free human life is absolutely full of funniness.

principles.statement: Individuals are limited by language, but they are not entirely determined by it.
principles.rationale: While words gain meaning through connections rather than just naming things, it is false to claim that a structural linguistic code is the only reality and that language speaks the individual.
principles.source_quote: Individuals are limited by their language. But this does not imply that there is a (postulated Hegelian) structure of language which alone is real and true.

principles.statement: Purity of genre still exists, and philosophy must not be fragmented.
principles.rationale: The post-structuralist idea that all genres are hybrid and fragmented poses a general danger to our civilization and undermines distinct disciplines.
principles.source_quote: Well, I don't agree. I think this is a very general danger to our civilization. Philosophy must not be fragmented into sociology

mental_models.name: The Novel as a Comic Form
mental_models.description: A lens for viewing the traditional novel not as a dense, poetic 'text' but as a reflection of ordinary human life, which is inherently comic and free.
mental_models.example: Contrasting the traditional novel, which embraces the profound funniness of human life, with the 'post-Derrida novel' where funniness has been killed.

signature_quotes.text: Funniness is also, I think, an aspect of freedom, something that totalitarian atmospheres can destroy.
signature_quotes.context: Discussing the comic nature of the traditional novel.

signature_quotes.text: I'm hostile to Derrida, I think that his influence has been damaging.
signature_quotes.context: Critiquing the impact of post-structuralism on young writers and critics.

signature_quotes.text: I think anyway that Derrida has no philosophy, it is a pseudo-philosophy; archi-écriture is a form of determinism.
signature_quotes.context: Arguing against the linguistic determinism of post-structuralist thought.

signature_quotes.text: Your individual meaning is taken away, you are a rendez-vous of codes.
signature_quotes.context: Describing the flaw in Derrida's view of language and the individual.

anti_patterns.description: Adopting Derridean post-structuralism in writing and criticism.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It frightens young writers away from traditional storytelling, forcing them into a constrained, dense, and overly poetic 'text' format that few can succeed at.

anti_patterns.description: Believing in linguistic determinism (that language speaks the individual).
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It strips away individual meaning and truth, reducing a person to a mere 'rendez-vous of codes' and creating a pseudo-philosophy that cannot account for its own truth.

anti_patterns.description: Accepting the fragmentation and hybridization of all genres.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It poses a general danger to civilization by undermining the purity and distinct purpose of disciplines, such as fragmenting philosophy into sociology.
